    "content": "You are an expert Linux kernel maintainer. Your goal is to perform a deep, rigorous review of a proposed kernel change to ensure safety, performance, and adherence to subsystem standards.

TOOL USAGE: When you need to gather information using tools, actively batch parallel or independent tool calls into a single response to minimize the number of conversation turns.

<global_review_guidelines>
The following documents contain the official technical patterns, architectural rules, and subsystem-specific guidelines that you MUST adhere to during your review. Use these as the absolute source of truth for identifying anti-patterns and violations.

@technical-patterns.md, @mm-reclaim.md

</global_review_guidelines>


Target Commit:
commit 2098d382a22f84095e63c001d082610fb5d2e85e
Author: Zhaoyang Huang <zhaoyang.huang@unisoc.com>
Date:   Wed Mar 18 01:15:58 2026 +0000

    mm: remove '!root_reclaim' checking in should_abort_scan()
    
    Android systems usually use memory.reclaim interface to implement user
    space memory management which expects that the requested reclaim target
    and actually reclaimed amount memory are not diverging by too much. With
    the current MGRLU implementation there is, however, no bail out when the
    reclaim target is reached and this could lead to an excessive reclaim
    that scales with the reclaim hierarchy size.For example, we can get a
    nr_reclaimed=394/nr_to_reclaim=32 proactive reclaim under a common 1-N
    cgroup hierarchy.
    This defect arised from the goal of keeping fairness among memcgs that
    is, for try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages -> shrink_node_memcgs ->
    shrink_lruvec -> lru_gen_shrink_lruvec -> try_to_shrink_lruvec, the
    !root_reclaim(sc) check was there for reclaim fairness, which was
    necessary before commit 'b82b530740b9' ("mm: vmscan: restore
    incremental cgroup iteration") because the fairness depended on
    attempted proportional reclaim from every memcg under the target
    memcg. However after commit 'b82b530740b9' there is no longer a need
    to visit every memcg to ensure fairness. Let's have try_to_shrink_lruvec
    bail out when the nr_reclaimed achieved.
    
    Suggested-by: T.J.Mercier <tjmercier@google.com>
    Reviewed-by: T.J.Mercier <tjmercier@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Zhaoyang Huang <zhaoyang.huang@unisoc.com>

diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
index 0fc9373e8251..10f1e7d716ca 100644
--- a/mm/vmscan.c
+++ b/mm/vmscan.c
@@ -4839,10 +4839,6 @@ static bool should_abort_scan(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)
 	int i;
 	enum zone_watermarks mark;
 
-	/* don't abort memcg reclaim to ensure fairness */
-	if (!root_reclaim(sc))
-		return false;
-
 	if (sc->nr_reclaimed >= max(sc->nr_to_reclaim, compact_gap(sc->order)))
 		return true;

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Removal of the `!root_reclaim(sc)` Check**

Okay, so I'm looking at this commit that removes the `!root_reclaim(sc)` check in `should_abort_scan()`. The explanation is that this check was originally about fairness in memory reclaim across memcgs, especially in a hierarchy. My initial thought is to refresh my memory on the specifics of memcg reclaim fairness and how it's handled.

The commit message says this check was specifically tied to how we used to handle proportional reclaim from every memcg under the target memcg before commit `b82b530740b9`. That commit changed things so we no longer have to visit *every* memcg to guarantee fairness; we can now bail out when the target reclaim amount (`nr_reclaimed`) is reached. That makes sense.

Let's dive into the code itself, `mm/vmscan.c`, and check `should_abort_scan()`.

```c
static bool should_abort_scan(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)
{
	int i;
	enum zone_watermarks mark;

	if (sc->nr_reclaimed >= max(sc->nr_to_reclaim, compact_gap(sc->order)))
		return true;

	/* check the order to exclude compaction-induced reclaim */
	if (!current_is_kswapd() || sc->order)
		return false;
...
```

The commit message explicitly states: "for try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages -> shrink_node_memcgs -> shrink_lruvec -> lru_gen_shrink_lruvec -> try_to_shrink_lruvec, the !root_reclaim(sc) check was there for reclaim fairness"

So, the key functions that use `should_abort_scan` are: `lru_gen_shrink_lruvec` and `lru_gen_scan_around`, which are called from the following: `try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages -> shrink_node_memcgs -> shrink_lruvec -> lru_gen_shrink_lruvec -> try_to_shrink_lruvec`.

To get a complete picture, I'm going to do a quick search within `mm/vmscan.c` for both `should_abort_scan` and `root_reclaim` to understand the whole context of the removal. That will help solidify my understanding of the changes and how they impact the overall reclaim strategy.

    "content": null,
    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analyzing the Abort Logic in `should_abort_scan`**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `should_abort_scan` function, and my initial thought was that removing the `root_reclaim(sc)` check opens the door for memcg reclaim to abort more readily. Now, I see I have to dig deeper.

Previously, with that check in place, memcg reclaim *always* returned `false` because `root_reclaim(sc)` would evaluate to false. Now, the logic flow is different, so let me parse through it.

I see that `sc->nr_reclaimed >= max(sc->nr_to_reclaim, compact_gap(sc->order))` is the first check. This means that if we've already reclaimed enough pages, we should abort, regardless of whether we're in direct or kswapd context or whatever. That makes sense.

Then comes the crux: `if (!current_is_kswapd() || sc->order) return false;`. Now, for memcg reclaim (which is often direct reclaim), `current_is_kswapd()` is typically `false`. Which means this condition will immediately return `false`. This line would be the blocker to allowing memcg reclaim to abort.

But now I remember the order of operations! The first check, `sc->nr_reclaimed >= ...`, gets a crack at returning `true` *before* we even hit the `current_is_kswapd()` check. This suggests the *intent* is to allow this early abort for direct reclaim if we've reclaimed enough.

Now, let's step back and consider what the code *used* to do. Before the patch, global direct reclaim *also* aborted when `nr_reclaimed >= nr_to_reclaim` because the `root_reclaim(sc)` check was true for that case.  The code flow for global direct reclaim was the same as the new code flow, meaning that this patch doesn't change the behavior for global direct reclaim. It simply adds the same functionality to memcg reclaim.

So the *net* effect of this patch is that memcg direct reclaim (and potentially other memcg reclaim) can *also* abort based on that same reclaimed-page count threshold. The function, with the patch, should only return false if kswapd is running or compaction-induced reclaim is active.

This seems intentional. The question is: Is this going to break something? We need to look at the *callers* of `should_abort_scan`. And MGLRU is one of them.

    "content": "{"output":"commit b82b530740b960eb949813043c4187c254039ac1\nAuthor: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\nDate:   Tue May 14 16:26:41 2024 -0400\n\n    mm: vmscan: restore incremental cgroup iteration\n    \n    Currently, reclaim always walks the entire cgroup tree in order to ensure\n    fairness between groups.  While overreclaim is limited in shrink_lruvec(),\n    many of our systems have a sizable number of active groups, and an even\n    bigger number of idle cgroups with cache left behind by previous jobs; the\n    mere act of walking all these cgroups can impose significant latency on\n    direct reclaimers.\n    \n    In the past, we've used a save-and-restore iterator that enabled\n    incremental tree walks over multiple reclaim invocations.  This ensured\n    fairness, while keeping the work of individual reclaimers small.\n    \n    However, in edge cases with a lot of reclaim concurrency, individual\n    reclaimers would sometimes not see enough of the cgroup tree to make\n    forward progress and (prematurely) declare OOM.  Consequently we switched\n    to comprehensive walks in 1ba6fc9af35b (\"mm: vmscan: do not share cgroup\n    iteration between reclaimers\").\n    \n    To address the latency problem without bringing back the premature OOM\n    issue, reinstate the shared iteration, but with a restart condition to do\n    the full walk in the OOM case - similar to what we do for memory.low\n    enforcement and active page protection.\n    \n    In the worst case, we do one more full tree walk before declaring\n    OOM. But the vast majority of direct reclaim scans can then finish\n    much quicker, while fairness across the tree is maintained:\n    \n    - Before this patch, we observed that direct reclaim always takes more\n      than 100us and most direct reclaim time is spent in reclaim cycles\n      lasting between 1ms and 1 second. Almost 40% of direct reclaim time\n      was spent on reclaim cycles exceeding 100ms.\n    \n    - With this patch, almost all page reclaim cycles last less than 10ms,\n      and a good amount of direct page reclaim finishes in under 100us. No\n      page reclaim cycles lasting over 100ms were observed anymore.\n    \n    The shared iterator state is maintaned inside the target cgroup, so\n    fair and incremental walks are performed during both global reclaim\n    and cgroup limit reclaim of complex subtrees.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20240514202641.2821494-1-hannes@cmpxchg.org\n    Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\n    Signed-off-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Reported-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Reviewed-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>\n    Reviewed-by: Roman Gushchin <roman.gushchin@linux.dev>\n    Cc: Facebook Kernel Team <kernel-team@fb.com>\n    Cc: Michal Hocko <mhocko@kernel.org>\n    Cc: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n"}",

    "thought": "**MGLRU Optimization: A Deep Dive into Early Scan Abort**

Okay, here's the deal: this patch aims to make MGLRU smarter about when to stop reclaiming memory. I've been tracing the logic through `should_abort_scan`, and a key change involves how it handles memcg reclaim. Let's break it down:

Previously, `should_abort_scan` simply returned `false` for memcg reclaim, effectively forcing the scan to continue until the bin was empty. Now, it includes a check: if `sc->nr_reclaimed` (the pages already reclaimed) is greater than or equal to `sc->nr_to_reclaim` (the target number of pages to reclaim), it aborts the scan. This is a crucial optimization to stop iterating memcgs once the target has been met.

The initial concern was that this might break the fairness of memcg reclaim. However, after careful analysis, it appears this isn't an issue. Since we're no longer dependent on visiting every memcg to ensure fairness (thanks to prior commit), this early abort doesn't harm anything. We're only stopping early if we've *achieved* our target, not unfairly skipping memcgs.

The `should_abort_scan` function is used by `evict_folios`, `shrink_many`, and `shrink_lruvec`.

Crucially, in `shrink_many`, the memcg iteration loop breaks if `should_abort_scan` returns `true`. The patch does what is intended, by allowing early bail out by iterating memcgs. `shrink_one` is called on the last shrunk memcg.

The crucial change is in `should_abort_scan` itself. The old check `!root_reclaim(sc)` unconditionally returns false.
The patch removes this, and it instead evaluates `nr_reclaimed >= max(sc->nr_to_reclaim, compact_gap(sc->order))`.

Let's also double-check potential edge cases:
-   **Kswapd and Memcg Reclaim:** `kswapd` strictly performs node-level reclaim, and never memcg reclaim. So the checks related to `current_is_kswapd()` are bypassed. So there is no conflict.
-   **`nr_to_reclaim` is 0:** The code ensures `nr_to_reclaim` is never 0 in practice.

In summary, this patch is a clear win. It optimizes MGLRU by enabling early scan abort in memcg reclaim without introducing any regressions or fairness issues. It's a clean and safe improvement to the memory management subsystem.

    "content": "{"output":"commit b82b530740b960eb949813043c4187c254039ac1\nAuthor: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\nDate:   Tue May 14 16:26:41 2024 -0400\n\n    mm: vmscan: restore incremental cgroup iteration\n    \n    Currently, reclaim always walks the entire cgroup tree in order to ensure\n    fairness between groups.  While overreclaim is limited in shrink_lruvec(),\n    many of our systems have a sizable number of active groups, and an even\n    bigger number of idle cgroups with cache left behind by previous jobs; the\n    mere act of walking all these cgroups can impose significant latency on\n    direct reclaimers.\n    \n    In the past, we've used a save-and-restore iterator that enabled\n    incremental tree walks over multiple reclaim invocations.  This ensured\n    fairness, while keeping the work of individual reclaimers small.\n    \n    However, in edge cases with a lot of reclaim concurrency, individual\n    reclaimers would sometimes not see enough of the cgroup tree to make\n    forward progress and (prematurely) declare OOM.  Consequently we switched\n    to comprehensive walks in 1ba6fc9af35b (\"mm: vmscan: do not share cgroup\n    iteration between reclaimers\").\n    \n    To address the latency problem without bringing back the premature OOM\n    issue, reinstate the shared iteration, but with a restart condition to do\n    the full walk in the OOM case - similar to what we do for memory.low\n    enforcement and active page protection.\n    \n    In the worst case, we do one more full tree walk before declaring\n    OOM. But the vast majority of direct reclaim scans can then finish\n    much quicker, while fairness across the tree is maintained:\n    \n    - Before this patch, we observed that direct reclaim always takes more\n      than 100us and most direct reclaim time is spent in reclaim cycles\n      lasting between 1ms and 1 second. Almost 40% of direct reclaim time\n      was spent on reclaim cycles exceeding 100ms.\n    \n    - With this patch, almost all page reclaim cycles last less than 10ms,\n      and a good amount of direct page reclaim finishes in under 100us. No\n      page reclaim cycles lasting over 100ms were observed anymore.\n    \n    The shared iterator state is maintaned inside the target cgroup, so\n    fair and incremental walks are performed during both global reclaim\n    and cgroup limit reclaim of complex subtrees.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20240514202641.2821494-1-hannes@cmpxchg.org\n    Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\n    Signed-off-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Reported-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Reviewed-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>\n    Reviewed-by: Roman Gushchin <roman.gushchin@linux.dev>\n    Cc: Facebook Kernel Team <kernel-team@fb.com>\n    Cc: Michal Hocko <mhocko@kernel.org>\n    Cc: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n"}",

Okay, so I've been looking at this patch that tweaks the behavior of MGLRU (Multi-Generation LRU) during memory reclaim, specifically around the `should_abort_scan` function and how it interacts with the reclamation target (`nr_to_reclaim`). The original code in `shrink_node_memcgs` that uses `mem_cgroup_iter` restored incremental cgroup iteration. However, I understand that MGLRU has its own iteration process, using `pgdat->memcg_lru` lists.

It turns out that MGLRU *does* have incremental iteration, albeit in a clever way. The `shrink_many` function, which is the core of MGLRU's reclaim process, picks a *random* bin within its generation to start iterating. Critically, and this is the crux of the incremental aspect, it rotates memcgs with `lru_gen_rotate_memcg` after being visited. That means if the scan is stopped part-way through – for whatever reason – the memcgs *not* visited in this pass will be at the head of the lists to be visited in the next iteration. This ensures that even if MGLRU aborts early, fairness is maintained, and all memcgs will get a chance to be considered over time.

Now, this patch removes the check that prevented it from aborting early within `should_abort_scan`.     "content": "{"content":"commit b82b530740b960eb949813043c4187c254039ac1\nAuthor: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\nDate:   Tue May 14 16:26:41 2024 -0400\n\n    mm: vmscan: restore incremental cgroup iteration\n    \n    Currently, reclaim always walks the entire cgroup tree in order to ensure\n    fairness between groups.  While overreclaim is limited in shrink_lruvec(),\n    many of our systems have a sizable number of active groups, and an even\n    bigger number of idle cgroups with cache left behind by previous jobs; the\n    mere act of walking all these cgroups can impose significant latency on\n    direct reclaimers.\n    \n    In the past, we've used a save-and-restore iterator that enabled\n    incremental tree walks over multiple reclaim invocations.  This ensured\n    fairness, while keeping the work of individual reclaimers small.\n    \n    However, in edge cases with a lot of reclaim concurrency, individual\n    reclaimers would sometimes not see enough of the cgroup tree to make\n    forward progress and (prematurely) declare OOM.  Consequently we switched\n    to comprehensive walks in 1ba6fc9af35b (\"mm: vmscan: do not share cgroup\n    iteration between reclaimers\").\n    \n    To address the latency problem without bringing back the premature OOM\n    issue, reinstate the shared iteration, but with a restart condition to do\n    the full walk in the OOM case - similar to what we do for memory.low\n    enforcement and active page protection.\n    \n    In the worst case, we do one more full tree walk before declaring\n    OOM. But the vast majority of direct reclaim scans can then finish\n    much quicker, while fairness across the tree is maintained:\n    \n    - Before this patch, we observed that direct reclaim always takes more\n      than 100us and most direct reclaim time is spent in reclaim cycles\n      lasting between 1ms and 1 second. Almost 40% of direct reclaim time\n      was spent on reclaim cycles exceeding 100ms.\n    \n    - With this patch, almost all page reclaim cycles last less than 10ms,\n      and a good amount of direct page reclaim finishes in under 100us. No\n      page reclaim cycles lasting over 100ms were observed anymore.\n    \n    The shared iterator state is maintaned inside the target cgroup, so\n    fair and incremental walks are performed during both global reclaim\n    and cgroup limit reclaim of complex subtrees.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20240514202641.2821494-1-hannes@cmpxchg.org\n    Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\n    Signed-off-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Reported-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Reviewed-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>\n    Reviewed-by: Roman Gushchin <roman.gushchin@linux.dev>\n    Cc: Facebook Kernel Team <kernel-team@fb.com>\n    Cc: Michal Hocko <mhocko@kernel.org>\n    Cc: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n"}",
    "role": "tool",
    "tool_call_id": "git_show"

My initial understanding was that `evict_folios` would scan that many entries, but that's not quite right. `evict_folios` stops when it either scans the full `nr_to_scan` *or* if it fails to find anything to scan (`!delta`).

The key lies in how the loop works.  `scanned` is incremented by `delta` after each call to `evict_folios`. The loop breaks if `scanned` is greater than or equal to `nr_to_scan`. So if `nr_to_scan` is much larger than `MAX_LRU_BATCH`, the loop can iterate. If `should_abort_scan` is true, the loop will terminate early, *between* batches, rather than continuing to the full `nr_to_scan`.

This is exactly how MGLRU breaks up the large reclaim into batches, allowing an abort *between* those batches. The `should_abort_scan` condition prevents a single memcg from consuming a huge amount of reclaim. Crucially, if `memcg_full_walk` is enabled, the code will still scan *at least* one batch from *every* memcg. This is critical for making forward progress across the whole memory tree.
